quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Correlation (r)"?

options.text: Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start
t-procedures are robust to moderate non-normality for large samples but can be misleading for small samples from highly skewed distributions
The difference between Q3 (75th percentile) and Q1 (25th percentile), measuring the spread of the middle 50% of data
Measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables; -1 ≤ r ≤ +1

quiz_questions.explanation: "Correlation (r)" is defined as: Measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables; -1 ≤ r ≤ +1. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Causation"?

options.text: A smooth curve that models the overall shape of a distribution; the total area under the curve equals 1 and represents probability
A change in x directly produces a change in y; requires ruling out confounding variables and establishing a mechanism
Standard computer output provides coefficients (b₀, b₁), standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and R² for interpretation
The long-run proportion of intervals that capture the true parameter; e.g., 95% means 95 out of 100 intervals would contain p

quiz_questions.explanation: "Causation" is defined as: A change in x directly produces a change in y; requires ruling out confounding variables and establishing a mechanism. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Confounding in regression"?

options.text: A third variable associated with both x and y that creates a spurious apparent relationship between them
Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start
A measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables; ranges from -1 to +1
When the effects of two variables on the response cannot be distinguished from each other because they are intertwined

quiz_questions.explanation: "Confounding in regression" is defined as: A third variable associated with both x and y that creates a spurious apparent relationship between them. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Establishing causation"?

options.text: The claim we suspect is true and are trying to find evidence for; can be one-sided () or two-sided (≠)
P(not A) = 1 - P(A); the probability that an event does NOT occur equals one minus the probability it does occur
Requires: (1) association, (2) temporal precedence (cause before effect), (3) elimination of confounders (typically through random assignment in experiments)
A variable that places individuals into groups or categories (e.g., gender, political party, blood type) and is summarized using counts or proportions

quiz_questions.explanation: "Establishing causation" is defined as: Requires: (1) association, (2) temporal precedence (cause before effect), (3) elimination of confounders (typically through random assignment in experiments). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Correlation and Causation, which best defines "Lurking variable"?

options.text: Tests H₀ that a categorical variable follows a specified distribution by comparing observed and expected counts
An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship
Using df = min(n₁-1, n₂-1) when technology is unavailable; produces wider intervals and larger p-values
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)

quiz_questions.explanation: In Correlation and Causation, "Lurking variable" refers to: An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables; -1 ≤ r ≤ +1"?

options.text: Union and intersection
Mutually exclusive events
Correlation (r)
Test statistic

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Correlation (r)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A change in x directly produces a change in y; requires ruling out confounding variables and establishing a mechanism"?

options.text: Coefficient of determination (r²)
Median
Causation
Coefficient of variation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Causation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A third variable associated with both x and y that creates a spurious apparent relationship between them"?

options.text: Independent events
Placebo
Association in two-way tables
Confounding in regression

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Confounding in regression".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Requires: (1) association, (2) temporal precedence (cause before effect), (3) elimination of confounders (typically through random assignment in experiments)"?

options.text: Coefficient of variation
Range
Establishing causation
Mutually exclusive events

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Establishing causation".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Correlation and Causation, which term matches: "An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship"?

options.text: Homogeneity vs. independence
Percentile
Lurking variable
Marginal distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: In Correlation and Causation, this refers to "Lurking variable".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Correlation and Causation is accurate?

options.text: Correlation and Causation is a critical distinction in statistics: correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, but it does not imply that one variable causes changes in the other.
Standardization and Z-Scores transform raw data values into a common scale by expressing each observation as the number of standard deviations it falls above or below the mean: z = (x - μ)/σ.
If the population has mean μ and standard deviation σ, then the sampling distribution of x̄ has mean μx̄ = μ and standard deviation σx̄ = σ/√n (the standard error).
Experiments use random assignment to eliminate confounding, allowing causal conclusions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Correlation and Causation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Correlation and Causation?

options.text: Understanding this distinction is critical—many important questions (smoking's effect on health, educational interventions) rely on observational data when experiments are unethical or impractical.
Interpreting Chi-Square Results requires understanding what a significant χ² value means and what it does not.
Observational data can reveal associations but not causation, because confounding variables may drive the apparent relationship.
The complement rule (P(Aᶜ) = 1 - P(A)), addition rule for unions, and multiplication rule for intersections form the core probability toolkit.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Correlation and Causation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Correlation and Causation?

options.text: Tree diagrams and two-way tables provide systematic visual methods for computing conditional probabilities in complex scenarios.
Controlling Variables involves design strategies to isolate the effect of the treatment variable by holding other variables constant, matching subjects, or using statistical adjustments.
The probability distribution table lists all possible values and their probabilities (which must sum to 1), enabling calculation of the mean μ = E(X) = Σ[x·P(x)] and variance σ² = Σ[(x-μ)²·P(x)].
Establishing causation requires controlled experiments with random assignment, ruling out confounders and reverse causality.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Correlation and Causation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Correlation and Causation?

options.text: Marginal distributions describe each variable alone (row or column totals divided by the grand total), while conditional distributions show the distribution of one variable within levels of the other.
Two-Way Tables and Conditional Distributions organize data for two categorical variables and provide the foundation for chi-square analysis.
A well-designed experiment with random assignment allows researchers to attribute differences in response to the treatment rather than confounding variables.

Correlation and Causation is a critical distinction in statistics: correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, but it does not imply that one variable causes changes in the other.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Correlation and Causation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Correlation and Causation?

options.text: Standardization and Z-Scores transform raw data values into a common scale by expressing each observation as the number of standard deviations it falls above or below the mean: z = (x - μ)/σ.
Observational data can reveal associations but not causation, because confounding variables may drive the apparent relationship.
Model comparison using r² and residual plots helps determine which model (linear, exponential, power) best fits the data.
Chi-Square Test for Independence tests whether two categorical variables are associated in a single population.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Correlation and Causation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Correlation and Causation?

options.text: The classic ice cream and drowning correlation: both increase in summer due to the lurking variable of temperature, not because ice cream causes drowning
Medical research blocking patients by disease severity before randomly assigning drug vs. placebo within each severity block
Healthcare studies using cluster sampling of hospitals rather than individual patients when a complete patient list is impractical
Pharmaceutical companies standardizing dosage timing, diet, and monitoring schedules across all treatment groups in a clinical trial

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Correlation and Causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Correlation and Causation?

options.text: Education policy comparing the distribution of reading proficiency levels (below, at, above grade level) across different school districts
Debunking the claim that countries with higher chocolate consumption produce more Nobel laureates by identifying wealth as the confounding variable
Laboratory experiments controlling temperature, humidity, and light exposure when testing plant growth under different fertilizer treatments
Analyzing the power relationship between body mass and metabolic rate in animals using log-log transformation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Correlation and Causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Correlation and Causation applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Medical researchers using randomized controlled trials rather than observational studies to establish whether a drug causes improvement, controlling for the placebo effect and confounders
Analyzing the relationship between study hours and exam scores to determine what proportion (r²) of score variation is explained by study time
Quality control calculating the probability of finding 0, 1, 2, or more defective items in a batch of 50 using the binomial distribution with known defect rate
Public health officials testing whether the proportion of residents with elevated lead levels differs from the national rate to justify environmental remediation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Correlation and Causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Correlation and Causation?

options.text: The classic ice cream and drowning correlation: both increase in summer due to the lurking variable of temperature, not because ice cream causes drowning
Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits
Manufacturing process monitoring using the sampling distribution of x̄ to set control chart limits for detecting when a process mean shifts
Political polling organizations using stratified sampling by demographic groups (age, region, party) to produce accurate election forecasts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Correlation and Causation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Correlation and Causation in practice?

options.text: Quality control in manufacturing using μ ± 3σ control limits to detect when a production process drifts out of specification
A manufacturer finding a significant chi-square result for defect type vs. production shift, then identifying that the night shift drives most of the association
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational
Debunking the claim that countries with higher chocolate consumption produce more Nobel laureates by identifying wealth as the confounding variable

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Correlation and Causation.
